sentences.text: Yes.
I forgot to mention it to you, too.
There's couple things which I never forget.
There were a lot of selections in our town.
One time, you know, my father came home, there's nobody to take-- to bury some people.
So my father took me to the Jewish cemetery.
There was selection, and they shot quite a few people.
So there were already a few people on the cemetery where they're digging out the graves for them.
There was one particular fellow, his name was Kopyto.
He used to-- he was a real Orthodox Jew, and he was in business.
He was wealthy man.
He used to sell liquor-- a liquor business.
I remember, I buried him.
When I buried him, he wear long coat with the fur inside.
When I tried to put him in a grave, because I couldn't because there were on top of us, the SS troopers-- you know, actually, they were the [NON-ENGLISH], we call them.
They were the ones that used to-- they were worse than the SS trooper.
They were Ukrainians.
But they become, in German, they become [NON-ENGLISH].
They wore black hats, with the [NON-ENGLISH], and the green uniform, and the black pants.
We called them [NON-ENGLISH].
They were corrupt.
They steal and rob.
You could have buy them off, if you could, you know?
So I remember I buried this particular gentleman, this fellow Kopyto, and I tried to take the coat off him, and got into snag and tried to pull it out.
All of a sudden, there was lot of dollar bills were coming with-- it was sewed up.
I buried him with everything, with all the money, with everything.
Poor fellow.
I felt so bad, because I was with his son in the camp and I told him about his dad.
I buried him myself.
There was another incident, for me.
It was a name-- her name was Gutfreind, Frau Gutfreind.
They used to have a-- they used to sell soft goods, material for clothes.
She was a older lady then, she was-- must've been in her 70s.
They took her to the woods, out of town, and they shot her.
So me and my friend, we were assigned to bury her down there, right there.
So after the war, when I was in Israel, I talked to her son, you know, about it.
She had a son, and the son had a grandson, lived in Israel.
I told him where she was buried.
So they went to Poland there, they got the remains, and buried her in the Jewish cemetery.
They found her, because I buried her down there myself.
Then I used to work for one fellow, his name-- not myself, there were bunch of us work for him-- we used to put his sign on the roads, like you see here, different sign.
His name was Scheda.
He was a German fellow from Sudetenland, from Czechoslovakia.
He was the worst fellow I ever seen, these days, before I went to camp.
He was such a sadist.
For no reason whatsoever, he used to beat us to beat the heck out of-- but he always used to like me, somehow.
I don't know why.
I had to go early in the morning to his house, in wintertime, make fire.
We had no heating like we have here, in United States.
Everything was burning wood in the stoves.
And I had to start the burning the wood.
I needed a truck to warm up, because it couldn't start it was so cold.
I used to polish his boots.
He always used to help me out.
To me he was very good, but he was such a sadist.
He used to give me a little bread, and stuff like that, help me out.
He was very good to me, as far as that.
But the others-- terrible.
Absolutely nothing.
They used to take the bodies to dump them in a grave and that's it.
Was no ceremony whatsoever.
Not only one.
Sometimes they buried, you know, a few at a time, because they used to do-- at the selection.
They used to take bunch of people to the cemetery, and they killed them, for no reason.
They had orders, I suppose, but there it was.
You know, and they killed them.
Before the ghetto.
Before the ghetto.
They used to come into the house, round them up at night, and take them out.
There was one lady, my mother's cousin.
Her name was Stattel, they had the jewelry store in my hometown.
Her husband ran away to Russia, and she had two sons-- three sons, my friends.
They took her and her mother.
She was old lady, must've been late 80s.
She could hardly walk.
They took them to the cemetery, they shot them, they killed them, buried in the same grave.
For no reason.
Yes.
Volksdeutsche, the Ukrainian-- Ukrainian.
They used to use them.
They were so corrupt.
They used to come through our Jewish homes, on their own, and used to destroy the home.
They were looking for gold, for coins, for gold, silver, anything like that.
They were so corrupt.
My father was killed when it was the final solution.
The last day when we left the town.
You know.
And my mother and sister went to the Belzec with the rest of the people in our town.
They load them up on a boxcar.
I got this from the-- from the Gentile people.
You know-- they load them on a boxcar.
They told them they're taking-- they're taking them to different camps.
Meantime they took them to Belzec, and my father, they finished him off right at the Jewish cemetery.
And my brother was still in Nowy Sacz, yes.
My brother was still in Nowy Sacz.
Yeah.
I'll tell you after a while what happened, when I came to Auschwitz.
But the-- but my father, when they brought him-- I gathered this from the people who used to live across the street from the cemetery.
They observed the whole thing, what was going on.
Like I said, they stripped them naked, they chase them all around the cemetery with their whips.
They beat them, then they line them up in front of the grave and machine gun them.
Some people that fell to the graves, maybe got hit in the arm and the legs, they just buried them alive.
I just hope my father was killed right away, didn't suffer.
I hope so.
I could tell you what happened-- about five years ago, I received a letter from the Polish government, from my local government, from my hometown.
And they send me pictures, also.
I have the pictures at home.
They would like to restore the grave.
But for all these years, for 50 years, nothing, but nothing grows on the grave-- and I have a picture of it.
Not even straw.
And their bones sticking out.
They'd like to put concrete slab, and they would like to put a marker on it.
They need money.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So there was four of us here in United States, from my friends, from my hometown.
So one lived in Chicago, one lived in New Jersey, in Newark, yes, and then one lived in California.
So we all want to get together, want to go back, because we were invited for the sermon after they pour the concrete, put in monument.
But meantime, we had to send them money, you know, which I did.
I could've buy the whole cemetery, what I sent them, which I don't care.
I did it for my dad, I did it for myself.
So I get-- a few months later, I get another letter then the-- they got everything ready.
They're going to put a monument now.
We'll be invited to come for the sermon.
So I get a letter, again, that things are ready to come down, and we were invited.
It was very nice letter.
And I was ready to go.
Got my passport, everything ready to go.
My friend from Chicago was going with me, and my friend from California, also.
Then I get another letter that they can guarantee me safety in my hometown.
We're going to stay in Zakopane, which was a neighboring town.
They're going to take us on a bus for the sermon, keep us on the buses.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So my wife didn't like the idea.
She starts-- she was worried.
She's-- you're not going to go, which I don't blame her, and I didn't want to go like that either.
And my friend from Chicago, too, he didn't want to go.
How can you go when they are guaranteeing your safety?
But my friend from California, he went.
When he arrive, they stole all his luggage.
The only thing he had what was on his back-- jogging suit.
As a matter of fact, I have video, they send me video from the sermon.
And few friends of ours came from Israel.
Was very nice sermon, but they kept him on a bus.
On the cemetery, they put a tent-- you can see the mayor of the town give speech, and sort of this and that, you know?
And they served him wine and the cookies after a while, and put him on the bus.
In our town, the mayor took him around the town, everything in the buses, on one bus.
And they show him the new hospital they built.
They're building it for 20 years.
The building had run out of money, so whoever was there they got together, they collect $1,000.
They give to the mayor the $1,000, because the mayor of the town promised he's going to take care of the grave, to help to build the hospital.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I never went back to Poland.
I would love to go back to see my dad grave.
I'd like to tell him something.
I wanted to tell him something.
I never had the chance in these days.
But now, things are a little bit different maybe.
So I would like to go back one time.
My wife don't want me to go.
Only thing I'm afraid to go myself.
If I go, I never come back-- because my heart-- you know, I had two heart attacks.
And it would be very hard for me to go back.
Why I'm going to go?
There's no Jewish people who live in that town, from this town, from 3,000 in a small town like that, with a beautiful synagogue.
They made a movie theater from our synagogue.
With Talmud Torah, a Jewish school, cheder.
You know, it was a very organized thing.
Not even a single Jew.
Right after liberation, after I came-- so six of us, friend of mine, where you going to go?
There's no place to go.
Want to go back home, look for-- I don't know what happened to my mother, to my sister.
I knew what happened to my dad, and I knew what happened to my brother, because Ihad seen what happened to my brother.
So I want to go back, look for relatives.
But I couldn't go, because I had to go to American consul with my signing the papers.
So six friend of mine went without me.
They didn't want to wait for me, not a day.
They went without me.
Soon as they arrive in Nowy Targ, stay overnight-- so the people came in, [NON-ENGLISH], they call themself Army [NON-ENGLISH], an organization, anti-Semite.
They killed them all.
They throw the bodies in the gutter, in the sidewalk, and put a sign on them-- Hitler won't finish you up, we will.
We don't want you here.
Right after the war, 1946.
So if I would've gone, probably I would've been one of them, too.
Let's go back to August 30, 1942.
And you were sent out from Nowy Targ.
I was sent out from Nowy Targ.
We left on the trucks, because it was only 13 miles-- 13 kilometers to Czarny Dunajec.
We all worked on it, for the Hobag worker.
So I worked on it from August till [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] probably around April or May, something like that.
I was sent out to Krakow-Plaszow.
Things were very bad for me in this camp.
It was a small camp.
I was away from home, I was a youngster, I need my family, I missed my family terrible.
My mother gave me two pair of pants to wear, and I had a hopsack.
And I wore these pants-- I remember I had blue corduroy pants I wore underneath.
I wore it so long till the corduroy wore out.
I was afraid to leave it, because somebody else would have maybe take it away from me.
I slept with them, I ate with them, and I work with it.
It was very hard camp.
It was the first camp away from home, and I knew I couldn't go back.
There's nobody to go back to.
There was no Jewish people anymore in town-- Judenrein they called.
There was no Jewish people in town.
And some people from my hometown, the Gentile people that work in this lumber yard, they used to travel by train for the Hobag company.
They got paid, naturally.
And then they were my neighbors, across the street.
One fellow, his name was Krupa, and I ask him-- could you bring me a piece of bread from home?
Could you help me out?
I'm starving here, because they don't feed us.
You know, we were away from home in the-- our ration was very poor.
In the morning, we used to get a slice of bread and a coffee.
Then lunchtime we got a little soup.
In evening again, a little bit soup, and slice of bread, and a black coffee.
They called coffee.
This was our diet.
But this camp, you know, from beginning was very, very hard on me.
You know, I wasn't used to-- I didn't know how to organize myself.
The most-- worst it was for me to miss my mother.
I was very attached to my mother.
I was very close with my mother, and I missed her terrible.
Even today.
I look at a picture, I talk to her.
I don't get no answer.
I miss her terrible.
You know, you go back, get old, you go back the way you started, they say.
And I miss her terrible.
Right now, I'm retired, I have more time, and I'm very depressed sometime, you know?
So in this camp, finally, I start to organize myself.
The-- the logger, the leader of our camp, on the Hobag worker, we used to call him-- his name was Schmidt.
He had a bunch of dogs, small dogs, little dogs like rats.
He used to-- he trained them specially-- they used to run after us to always bite you on your legs, on your pants.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
One time, one of his dog died.
So I was selected, and my friend in Israel, to bury this dog, and take the skin of the dog and bring them over to him-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to the-- to Mr.
Schmidt, Herr Schmidt.
So we did it.
So what I did, we took the meat.
Me and my friend, we buried underneath the barracks.
I was very happy, because every day I used to go cut a-- we had no knife but a spoon-- I had a spoon-- so spoon was very important to have in the camp.
Used to sharpen the spoon so it was like a knife.
Used to cut piece of meat, used to bring to the barracks and cook at night-- that extra little something to eat.
And I brought this-- the skin from the-- the pelt from the dog.
I don't know what he did with it, but this kind of guy, he was-- he was a cruel man.
He was in charge of the whole industry.
Running the camp, they were Gestapo.
Gestapo, yes.
And the werkschutz fuhrer-- the werkschutz.
They were the police for the work-- for the company.
They called them werkschutz.
Privately-owned company, yes.
Never receive a dime.
I don't know if they're still in businesses or not.
They used to be-- I think the main headquarter must've been in the-- in the Silesia, [NON-ENGLISH], down there some place.
Because we used to build the prefab homes.
Used to send to Germany, because Germany used to be bombed by the Allied forces, and we used to build the homes all the time for them.
I become a carpenter, after a while.
OK.
How many people from Nowy Targ were with you do you know?
60, there were 60 of us.
Of course.
We all knew each other.
Yes.
How long--
In the camp?
Till April.
1943, Yeah.
'43, Yeah.
Krakow-Plaszow, yes.
I came to Plaszow.
We build the camp on the Jewish cemetery.
The camp was built on a Jewish-- there was a big cemetery, Plaszow.
So we-- I-- because we were like a carpenter, so we were in they called the [NON-ENGLISH] commando.
We build the barracks.
So what we did, we level out the ground, we dig out the grave, knock the stones-- headstones, we dig out the graves.
Meantime, you come across, you know, skeletons.
I'm ashamed to say, but I can't help myself.
I used to live on that too many times-- sometimes we pick out the gold teeth from the skeleton.
And I used to trade it with the Ukrainians.
They used to bring us bread.
They was so corrupt.
You could've buy them-- if you had money, or you had something gold, or something like used to-- they did you favor.
They used to come, they used to watch us.
And they were behind the fences.
We used to get with each other, used to trade in the gold for a slice of bread, something like that.
So we used to build the barracks.
Who was in charge at Krakow-Plaszow?
We were divided out into groups.
Are you talking about when we went to work or the camp?
The camp was Amon Goeth.
He was the-- he was the camp-- commandant, yes.
And the Jewish commandant in the camp was Cholovich.
He was a Jewish policeman, OD men-- OD men-- OD men, they-- we used to call them.
They wear uniforms-- like-- and with the yellow hair-- yellow mark around their heads.
He was something else, too.
When we arrived to Krakow-Plaszow, there was one OD man named Finkelstein.
He came in with the whip.
Right away, start to call us name.
He was worse than the Nazi.
He was worse than the SS troopers.
Start to call us name.
I'm ashamed to say, what a name he called.
[NON-ENGLISH], it's a Polish name.
You son of a bitches, you come over here.
Don't think you're on vacation.
And he used to beat on us.
He used to whip us.
You know?
We didn't get the-- when we were in Czarny Dunajec, nobody beat us.
Once we did-- we perform our work, and that's it.
You know, once in a while, somebody got-- because we didn't have no-- the Ukrainian watch us.
That's-- their own Gestapo used to come around, and they had their own police, werkschutz.
There were pretty nice to us.
There were a couple of them, that wasn't so bad.
Was a small, very small camp.
There were 60 of us was, that's all.
But when we came to Plaszow, was a different story.
There were some people already there-- not too many.
We start to build the barracks.
And we built the barracks.
Then they bring the people from the ghetto, they liquidated ghetto in Krakow.
They brought them to Plaszow.
And this commander Goeth, he was unbelievable.
He was tall man, handsome man-- good-looking man.
Always used to ride on a white horse, and his girlfriend, again, on a black horse.
She always wore white turtleneck.
I never forget.
She was good-looking woman.
I don't know if she was German or she was Polish-- I don't know who she was, really.
But every time he was riding, we had to stand up-- whatever you did, you'd stand up.
And they called, [NON-ENGLISH], and bow down to him, and report to him.
If he didn't like something, he'd stop by and many times I've seen he took the pistol and kill people for no reason, just like that.
This was his satisfaction.
Friend of mine, lives in Israel, his name is Jacob Schneider-- he still lives, I just saw him a few months ago.
He used to work for him in his home-- he used to take care of his dogs.
He took care of his dogs.
So he used to feed them the best kind of meat.
After work, he used to come to our barracks.
I slept in the barracks number 18 in Plaszow.
He used to bring meat, used to help us out-- his close friends.
He took care of his dogs, Goeth's dogs-- he had a bunch of dogs.
I never forget-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when they brought a guy-- he tried to escape, a young fellow.
They brought him into Krakow-Plaszow, and the whole camp-- the whole population together-- watch him hang, to be hang.
So they had-- we all watch, stand and watch him.
First time they hang him, the rope broke, fell down.
Three times, the rope broke down, they couldn't hang him.
Finally he got so mad, Goeth, himself, took the pistol and-- when the rope broke down, he fell down, he shot him right there.
This was incident.
Second time I remember, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had a-- the fellow who used to-- the engineer, they called engineer, he would set up the plan to where to put up the barracks.
His name was Krakauer, a Jewish fellow.
And he had a group with him, they used to work with him.
He was the engineer.
He was setting up the plans.
Krakauer, he was older man.
And after the camps was finished almost, he brought all the groups-- his group-- to Commander Goeth.
He didn't like something, it was they did wrong.
They put them in a circle, hold each other.
There were a bunch of them.
They brought the steam shovel, they buried them alive in Plaszow-- right by the [NON-ENGLISH] station down there.
